threats.title: release() Does Not Update userTotalStaked, Permanently Locking Released Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The release() function restores slashedAmount back to stake.amount and decrements totalSlashed, but never increments userTotalStaked[staker]. Since slash() decremented userTotalStaked, the value is permanently deflated after a successful appeal. When the user attempts to withdraw the released amount, the subtraction userTotalStaked[msg.sender] -= amount in withdrawSelfStake/withdrawCommunityStake underflows and reverts, permanently locking the released funds in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct accounting bug confirmed by code inspection. Every other function that modifies stake.amount also updates userTotalStaked symmetrically (selfStake, communityStake, withdrawSelfStake, withdrawCommunityStake, slash), but release() is the sole exception. This violates the protocol's stated invariant that userTotalStaked equals the sum of all active stakes.

proof_of_signal.lines: release function body, after selfStakes[staker].amount += amountToRelease and communityStakes[staker][stakee].amount += amountToRelease
proof_of_signal.pattern: stake.amount is increased but userTotalStaked[staker] is never updated; compare with slash() which decrements both

preconditions: A user has an active stake (self or community)
SLASHER_ROLE slashes the stake
RELEASER_ROLE calls release() for any amount
User later attempts to withdraw the full restored amount

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: IdentityStaking.sol release() function, both if/else branches
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: stake.amount += amountToRelease without corresponding userTotalStaked[staker] += amountToRelease
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After slash(100→50, userTotalStaked=50) then release(50→100, userTotalStaked still 50), withdraw(100) fails: 50-100 underflows. User can only withdraw 50, losing 50 GTC permanently.

analysis_questions: Can the user partially withdraw to reduce loss, or is the full released amount locked?
Does re-staking on top of the desynchronized state allow recovery?
How many historical release() calls have occurred in production?
validation_requirements: Trace: selfStake(100) → slash(50%) → release(50) → withdrawSelfStake(100) → verify revert
Confirm userTotalStaked is only decremented in slash, never incremented in release
Verify this affects both self-stake and community-stake release paths

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No active attacker needed. Any legitimate slash+release cycle on any user triggers permanent fund lockup. The released amount minus the still-tracked userTotalStaked becomes permanently unwithdrawable. Only a contract upgrade could recover funds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All released slash amounts are permanently locked. Magnitude depends on total slashed-then-released volume.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Every user who successfully appeals a slash and receives a release
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Contract upgrade required to fix accounting or add manual withdrawal

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covering full slash → release → withdraw lifecycle

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - any staked amount triggers the bug after slash+release
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction: slash in one tx, release in another, withdraw attempt later
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable on-chain by comparing userTotalStaked to sum of stake amounts after any release

threats.title: uint88 Intermediate Overflow in slash() Makes Large Stakes Un-slashable at High Percentages
threats.hypothesis_description: In slash(), the expression (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100 performs uint88 × uint88 multiplication. Since both operands are uint88 in Solidity 0.8.23, the intermediate product is computed in uint88 and reverts on overflow. For percent=100 and amount > type(uint88).max/100 ≈ 3.09M GTC, the multiplication overflows, causing the entire slash transaction to revert. An attacker can stake >3.09M GTC to become immune to high-percentage slashing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code declares percent as uint88 (not uint256), and stake.amount is uint88 from the Stake struct. Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic will revert when the intermediate uint88 result exceeds 2^88-1. The code comment acknowledges uint88 holds 300M tokens but ignores the multiplication intermediate.

proof_of_signal.lines: slash() self-staker loop: uint88 slashedAmount = (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100; and community-staker loop: uint88 slashedAmount = (percent * communityStakes[staker][stakee].amount) / 100;
proof_of_signal.pattern: uint88 * uint88 intermediate overflow; percent(uint88) * amount(uint88) exceeds uint88 max for large stakes

preconditions: Staker has >3.09M GTC staked (at 100% slash) or >6.18M GTC (at 50% slash)
SLASHER_ROLE attempts to slash at a percent where percent * amount > type(uint88).max
GTC supply and market allows accumulation of that amount

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: IdentityStaking.sol slash() function, both loop bodies
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uint88 slashedAmount = (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100 where both operands are uint88
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: type(uint88).max ≈ 3.09×10^26. At percent=100, amount=4M×10^18=4×10^24: 100×4×10^24=4×10^26 > 3.09×10^26 → revert. Fix: cast to uint256 before multiplication.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No uint256 cast on multiplication operands

analysis_questions: What is the exact threshold for each slash percentage (1-100)?
Can the SLASHER work around this by doing multiple low-percentage slashes?
Does the slash array processing revert the entire batch or just skip the overflowing entry?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test: selfStake(3.1M GTC), then slash([staker], [], [], 100) → verify revert
Calculate exact breakpoint: type(uint88).max / 100 = threshold amount
Verify that slashing the entire batch reverts (not just one entry)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker acquires >3.1M GTC, stakes it all via selfStake. When identified as Sybil, SLASHER attempts slash(100%). Transaction reverts due to intermediate overflow. Attacker is effectively immune to meaningful slashing. SLASHER must resort to many 1% slashes (compounding, not additive), requiring ~70 transactions for equivalent of 50% total slash. Each slash reverts the entire batch if any single address overflows.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Core slashing mechanism defeated for large stakers; Sybil resistance broken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users rely on slashing for trust/reputation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Contract upgrade to cast to uint256 before multiplication

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with stake amounts above 3M GTC at high slash percentages

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Not applicable to the overflow itself
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single stake transaction, then immune to high-% slashes indefinitely
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain: large staker cannot be slashed, slash txs revert

threats.title: withdrawSelfStake Missing Zero-Amount Check Allows No-Op Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawSelfStake() does not validate that amount > 0, unlike withdrawCommunityStake() which explicitly checks. This allows zero-amount withdrawals that emit misleading SelfStakeWithdrawn events and execute zero-value token transfers, creating event log pollution and inconsistency between paired operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Parallel code path comparison: withdrawCommunityStake has 'if (amount == 0) { revert AmountMustBeGreaterThanZero(); }' but withdrawSelfStake lacks this check entirely. This is an inconsistency in the symmetric withdraw operations.

proof_of_signal.lines: withdrawSelfStake() function entry - no amount > 0 check
proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing validation present in parallel function withdrawCommunityStake; amount=0 passes all checks (0 > sStake.amount is false)

preconditions: User has any self-stake with unlockTime <= block.timestamp
Contract is not paused

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: withdrawSelfStake(0) succeeds, emitting SelfStakeWithdrawn(msg.sender, 0) and calling token.transfer(msg.sender, 0)

analysis_questions: Can the zero-value token.transfer cause any unexpected behavior with specific ERC20 implementations?
Do off-chain indexers or UIs misinterpret zero-amount withdrawal events?
validation_requirements: Call withdrawSelfStake(0) on an unlocked stake and verify it succeeds
Confirm event emission with zero amount

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any user can spam zero-amount withdrawals to pollute event logs. Limited financial impact but demonstrates code inconsistency that could mask deeper issues.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Add zero-amount check in contract upgrade

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Minimal gas cost per spam call

threats.title: Duplicate Addresses in slash() Arrays Cause Compounding Slashes Beyond Intended Percentage
threats.hypothesis_description: The slash() function iterates over address arrays without deduplication. If the same address appears multiple times in selfStakers or the same (staker,stakee) pair appears multiple times in communityStakers/communityStakees, each iteration slashes the remaining balance. Two entries at 50% result in 75% total slash (50% of 100 + 50% of 50), not the intended 50%.
threats.investigation_rationale: The slash function loops over arrays directly from calldata without checking for duplicates. Since SLASHER_ROLE is semi-trusted (off-chain analysis system), a bug in the off-chain system or a compromised/negligent slasher could submit duplicate addresses, causing more funds to be slashed than the intended percentage.

proof_of_signal.lines: slash() both for-loops over selfStakers and communityStakers arrays
proof_of_signal.pattern: No deduplication check; each iteration reads current stake.amount which was reduced by previous iteration

preconditions: SLASHER_ROLE submits arrays containing duplicate addresses (intentionally or via off-chain bug)
Target has sufficient stake balance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: slashedAmount = (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100 reads updated amount on each iteration
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: selfStakers=[alice,alice] with 50%: first pass slashes 50, second pass slashes 25 = 75% total

analysis_questions: Is there off-chain validation preventing duplicates?
Does the round-tracking logic behave correctly on the second iteration for the same address?
Can this cause totalSlashed desync?
validation_requirements: Test: slash([alice, alice], [], [], 50) with alice staking 100 → verify total slashed is 75, not 50
Verify accounting remains consistent (totalSlashed, userTotalStaked)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised or buggy off-chain slashing system submits duplicate addresses, causing users to lose more funds than the intended slash percentage. Users have no on-chain recourse aside from the appeal process.
prerequisites: SLASHER_ROLE submits duplicates in calldata arrays

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users slashed for more than intended percentage; up to 100% of stake could be slashed at lower nominal percentages
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any staker targeted by the duplicate slash
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: RELEASER_ROLE could release excess, but requires detecting the error

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with duplicate addresses in slash arrays

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing slash events per address per transaction

threats.title: Missing Storage Gap (__gap) in Upgradeable Contract Risks Storage Collision on Upgrade
threats.hypothesis_description: IdentityStaking is a UUPS upgradeable contract but does not declare a __gap storage variable. If a future upgrade adds new state variables between existing ones or if a child contract inherits IdentityStaking and adds its own state, storage slots could collide, corrupting critical financial data (stake amounts, slash tracking, role assignments).
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard OpenZeppelin guidance for upgradeable contracts requires __gap arrays to reserve storage slots. The Upgrade.sol test contract inherits IdentityStaking, demonstrating the inheritance pattern is used. Any future upgrade adding state variables could silently corrupt existing storage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: UUPS upgradeable contract without uint256[N] __gap storage reservation

preconditions: A future upgrade adds new state variables to IdentityStaking
DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE authorizes the upgrade

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No uint256[50] private __gap or similar gap variable
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: New variables in upgraded implementation could overwrite existing mappings or role data

analysis_questions: What is the exact storage layout of the current IdentityStaking implementation?
Does Upgrade.sol introduce any new storage variables?
Could the OZ base contracts' gaps protect against some collisions?
validation_requirements: Generate storage layout and verify no current collisions
Simulate adding a new variable in an upgrade and check for corruption
Verify OZ base contracts include their own gaps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin unknowingly deploys an upgraded implementation with a new state variable. The variable overlaps with existing storage (e.g., totalSlashed mapping or token address), corrupting financial accounting. Users' stakes could be misdirected or lost.
prerequisites: Future upgrade with additional state variables

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential corruption of all financial state (stakes, slashes, token address)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires another upgrade to fix, but corrupted data may be unrecoverable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by storage layout comparison tools before upgrade

threats.title: lockAndBurn Does Not Zero totalSlashed for Burned Round, Leaving Stale Accounting Data
threats.hypothesis_description: After lockAndBurn transfers tokens for a burned round, totalSlashed[roundToBurn] retains its pre-burn value instead of being zeroed. While no current code path re-reads this stale value for financial operations, it creates inaccurate on-chain accounting that could mislead off-chain systems and becomes a latent vulnerability if future upgrades reference historical round data.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lockAndBurn function reads amountToBurn = totalSlashed[roundToBurn], transfers tokens, and emits Burn event, but never sets totalSlashed[roundToBurn] = 0. The stale slash handling in slash() zeros individual stake slashedAmounts for old rounds but does not decrement the corresponding totalSlashed entries.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalSlashed[roundToBurn] is read but never zeroed after successful burn transfer

preconditions: At least one lockAndBurn has been executed with non-zero slashed amount
Off-chain system or future upgrade reads totalSlashed for burned rounds

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Stale data persists; totalSlashed[burnedRound] still shows pre-burn value
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cleanup of burned round data

analysis_questions: Does any current function path read totalSlashed for rounds < currentSlashRound - 1?
Could a future upgrade accidentally use stale data for double-burning?
Do off-chain indexers rely on totalSlashed accuracy?
validation_requirements: Verify totalSlashed[burnedRound] > 0 after lockAndBurn
Confirm no current function re-reads stale values
Assess off-chain integration dependencies

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Currently no direct financial exploit. Risk materializes if a future upgrade introduces logic that reads historical totalSlashed values, potentially triggering double-burns or incorrect accounting.
prerequisites: Future code change referencing historical totalSlashed

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Currently cosmetic; potential future double-burn risk

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying totalSlashed is zeroed after burn

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable by reading totalSlashed for past rounds

threats.title: Integer Truncation in slash() Allows Micro-Stakes to Evade Slashing While Corrupting Round Metadata
threats.hypothesis_description: When (percent * stake.amount) / 100 truncates to zero (e.g., 1% of 99 wei = 0), the slash still updates slashedInRound to currentSlashRound and adds 0 to slashedAmount. This metadata pollution can trigger round migration logic on subsequent slashes, moving existing slashed amounts between rounds without adding new slashes, potentially delaying burns.
threats.investigation_rationale: The slash function unconditionally sets slashedInRound and processes round migration before computing slashedAmount. Even when slashedAmount = 0, the metadata is updated. A dust staker can have their slashedInRound set without losing any tokens, interfering with future slash/release operations.

proof_of_signal.lines: slash() loop bodies: slashedAmount calculation and subsequent unconditional metadata updates
proof_of_signal.pattern: (percent * amount) / 100 rounds to 0 for small amounts; slashedInRound still updated

preconditions: Stake amount is small enough that percent * amount < 100
SLASHER_ROLE includes the dust staker in a slash batch

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: IdentityStaking.sol slash() loop: uint88 slashedAmount = (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No check for slashedAmount == 0 before updating metadata
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: slashedInRound is set even when no tokens are actually slashed; triggers round migration for 0 amounts

analysis_questions: Can zero-amount slashes be used to repeatedly migrate existing slashed amounts to new rounds, delaying burns?
Does the metadata corruption affect the release function's round validation?
What is the minimum stake amount that avoids truncation for each slash percentage?
validation_requirements: Test slash at 1% on a 99 wei stake and verify slashedAmount = 0 but slashedInRound updated
Verify round migration with zero amounts doesn't corrupt totalSlashed
Test release after zero-amount slash

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates many dust stakes (99 wei each). When SLASHER includes them in slash batches, zero tokens are slashed but round metadata is corrupted. If combined with other stakes from previous rounds, the round migration moves real slashed amounts forward, potentially delaying burns beyond the appeal window.
prerequisites: Stake amount < 100 wei per unit of slash percent

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Dust stakers immune to low-% slashing; potential burn delay
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Use 100% slash for dust stakes; add zero-check in upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for sub-100-wei stakes under low-percentage slashing

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring Slash events with amount=0

threats.title: burnAddress Misconfiguration Can Permanently DoS lockAndBurn, Blocking Round Progression
threats.hypothesis_description: The initialize() function does not validate burnAddress. If burnAddress is set to address(0) and the GTC token reverts on zero-address transfers (as the production GTC does), lockAndBurn will permanently revert whenever amountToBurn > 0, blocking all future round progression and preventing any further slashed funds from being burned.
threats.investigation_rationale: The initialize function validates tokenAddress != address(0) but does NOT validate burnAddress. The deployment script (deploy.ts) deploys a 'Lock' contract, not IdentityStaking, providing no evidence of burnAddress validation. GTC's _transferTokens requires dst != address(0).
deployment_assumptions: burnAddress is set to a valid non-zero address that can receive GTC transfers
threats.config_evidence: IdentityStaking.sol initialize(): no validation on _burnAddress parameter; deploy.ts does not deploy IdentityStaking, so no deployment evidence of burnAddress value

proof_of_signal.lines: initialize() - burnAddress = _burnAddress with no validation; lockAndBurn() - token.transfer(burnAddress, amountToBurn)
proof_of_signal.pattern: No address(0) check on burnAddress; if set to 0, token.transfer to zero address reverts in most ERC20s

preconditions: burnAddress set to address(0) or an address that cannot receive tokens
Any non-zero slash exists in a round
lockAndBurn is called

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: GTC._transferTokens requires dst != address(0); lockAndBurn calls token.transfer(burnAddress, amount)

analysis_questions: What burnAddress was used in actual deployment?
Can burnAddress be changed post-deployment? (No setter found)
Does the GTC token on mainnet/Optimism revert on zero-address transfers?
validation_requirements: Verify no setter for burnAddress exists (requires upgrade to change)
Test lockAndBurn with burnAddress=address(0) and amountToBurn > 0
Check production deployment burnAddress value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If burnAddress is misconfigured at deployment, all lockAndBurn calls revert when slashed amount > 0. Round progression halts, slashed amounts cannot be burned, and new slashing rounds cannot begin. Only a contract upgrade can fix this.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Slashing mechanism completely disabled; slashed tokens cannot be burned
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Contract upgrade required to fix burnAddress

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable immediately on first lockAndBurn attempt

threats.title: No Reentrancy Guard on Token Transfer Functions Relies on CEI Pattern Stability
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw functions (withdrawSelfStake, withdrawCommunityStake) and staking functions (selfStake, communityStake) perform external token transfers without reentrancy guards. While the current code follows checks-effects-interactions (CEI) pattern correctly, this is fragile against future code modifications in upgrades. If any upgrade adds state reads after the external call or reorders operations, reentrancy becomes possible.

proof_of_signal.lines: withdrawSelfStake, withdrawCommunityStake, selfStake, communityStake, lockAndBurn - external calls to token
proof_of_signal.pattern: External calls (token.transfer, token.transferFrom) without nonReentrant modifier; relies solely on CEI ordering

preconditions: Token contract has callback mechanism (ERC-777 or malicious token)
OR future upgrade reorders state changes relative to external calls
Contract is not paused

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: All functions with token.transfer or token.transferFrom calls
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No ReentrancyGuard / nonReentrant modifier on any function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: CEI is correctly followed currently but fragile; GTC is standard ERC20 without callbacks, so not currently exploitable

analysis_questions: Does the production GTC token have any callback mechanisms?
Could a future token migration introduce callbacks?
Is the CEI pattern maintained in all current code paths?
validation_requirements: Verify GTC token does not implement ERC-777 or similar callback standard
Verify CEI ordering in all functions with external calls
Assess risk of future upgrade breaking CEI

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not currently exploitable with standard GTC. If token is ever changed to one with transfer hooks (via upgrade), an attacker could reenter withdraw functions to drain funds. Defense in depth recommends nonReentrant modifier.
prerequisites: Token with callback mechanism or future code change

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential fund drainage if reentrancy becomes possible

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy test with callback tokens

threats.title: Pause Blocks Withdrawals Alongside Deposits, Enabling Indefinite Fund Lockup by PAUSER_ROLE
threats.hypothesis_description: All user-facing functions including withdrawSelfStake and withdrawCommunityStake use the whenNotPaused modifier. A compromised or malicious PAUSER_ROLE can indefinitely pause the contract, preventing all users from withdrawing their funds. There is no emergency withdrawal mechanism that bypasses the pause state.

proof_of_signal.lines: withdrawSelfStake: whenNotPaused modifier; withdrawCommunityStake: whenNotPaused modifier
proof_of_signal.pattern: All withdrawal functions gated by whenNotPaused; no emergency withdrawal bypass

preconditions: PAUSER_ROLE pauses the contract
PAUSER_ROLE key is lost, compromised, or acts maliciously
DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE cannot or does not revoke PAUSER_ROLE and unpause in time

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: whenNotPaused on withdrawal functions with no emergency bypass
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If paused indefinitely, all staked GTC is permanently locked; DEFAULT_ADMIN could upgrade but this is a complex recovery
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No emergency withdrawal function, no time-limited pause

analysis_questions: Can DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE unpause directly, or only through PAUSER_ROLE?
Is there a timelock or maximum pause duration?
Could a UUPS upgrade bypass the pause?
validation_requirements: Verify DEFAULT_ADMIN can grant itself PAUSER_ROLE and unpause
Confirm no maximum pause duration exists
Test upgrade while paused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised PAUSER_ROLE pauses contract. If DEFAULT_ADMIN cannot quickly respond, all staked funds are locked. Even with DEFAULT_ADMIN response, the window of fund lockup could be significant.
prerequisites: PAUSER_ROLE key compromise or malicious action

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All staked GTC locked for duration of pause
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: DEFAULT_ADMIN can grant PAUSER_ROLE to new address and unpause, or upgrade contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pause + withdrawal attempt + recovery flow

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Instant pause, recovery depends on admin response
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately visible on-chain via Paused event

threats.title: GTC Test Token transferFrom Bypasses Allowance Check Allowing Unauthorized Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The GTC test token's transferFrom function has the allowance check commented out ('// Removed allowance check for easy testing'). Any address can transfer any other address's GTC tokens without approval. While explicitly marked as test code, if this mock were accidentally deployed in a staging or production environment alongside IdentityStaking, any user's staking tokens could be stolen.
threats.investigation_rationale: The transferFrom function performs _transferTokens(src, dst, amount) without checking or decrementing allowances[src][msg.sender]. This is an intentional test simplification but represents a complete ERC-20 compliance failure.
deployment_assumptions: GTC.sol is only used in testing and never deployed to production
threats.config_evidence: GTC.sol header: 'THIS CONTRACT IS ONLY USED AS PART OF TESTING / THIS IS NOT PRODUCTION CODE'. deploy.ts deploys 'Lock' not GTC. Production uses real GTC token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Comment: '// Removed allowance check for easy testing'; _transferTokens called without allowance verification

preconditions: GTC.sol test mock is deployed instead of production GTC token
Users hold GTC tokens in accounts

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No allowance check or decrement; direct _transferTokens(src, dst, amount)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can call transferFrom(victim, attacker, victimBalance) to steal all tokens

analysis_questions: Is there any risk of the test GTC being used in staging/testnet with real value?
Does the test suite adequately distinguish test tokens from production?
Could CI/CD accidentally deploy this mock?
validation_requirements: Verify production deployment uses real GTC, not this mock
Confirm test environments don't hold value
Review deployment scripts for GTC references

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the test GTC mock is accidentally used in production, any attacker can drain any user's GTC balance by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, amount) without any prior approval.
prerequisites: Test GTC deployed to production or value-bearing environment

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of all user GTC balances

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking allowance before transferFrom
